<br clear="all" style="page-break-before:always;">
<div style="font-family:Times New Roman;">
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;"><a href="#TableOfContents" title="Click to go to Table of Contents">Table of Contents</a></font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt 1.25in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt 1.25in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">efficient path to product candidate development, registration and commercialization.&#160; We believe that TSR-011 is representative of this type of asset as are certain of our immunotherapy product candidates.</font></p>
<p align="center" style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-align:center;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt 1.25in;text-indent:-.25in;"><font size="2" face="Symbol" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&#183;</font><font size="1" style="font-size:3.0pt;">&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160; </font><i><font size="2" style="font-size:10.0pt;font-style:italic;">Currently marketed products and soon to be marketed products around which we could develop a commercial operation.</font></i><font size="2" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&#160; We continue to seek, and may in-license or acquire, assets that have received regulatory approval and are, or are about to be, marketed to the same treatment centers and healthcare professionals as those to whom we would market our product candidates, if approved.&#160; Having multiple marketed products can lead to efficiencies of scale in sales and marketing and medical affairs, and can drive faster market penetration for future products.</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt 1.25in;text-indent:-.25in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt .75in;text-indent:-.25in;"><font size="2" face="Symbol" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&#183;</font><font size="1" style="font-size:3.0pt;">&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160; </font><b><i><font size="2" style="font-size:10.0pt;font-style:italic;font-weight:bold;">Build Global Capabilities to Maximize the Value of Our Product Candidates.&#160; </font></i></b><font size="2" style="font-size:10.0pt;">We currently retain the exclusive worldwide rights to all of our current product candidates (rolapitant, niraparib, TSR-011, and any future product candidates which may result from our collaboration with </font><font size="2" style="font-size:10.0pt;">AnaptysBio</font><font size="2" style="font-size:10.0pt;">), and we intend to utilize these rights to develop and commercialize our product candidates globally.&#160; We will seek to acquire global rights for product candidates we may acquire or in-license in the future.</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt .75in;text-indent:-.25in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt 1.25in;text-indent:-.25in;"><font size="2" face="Symbol" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&#183;</font><font size="1" style="font-size:3.0pt;">&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160; </font><i><font size="2" style="font-size:10.0pt;font-style:italic;">Develop Our Products Globally.</font></i><font size="2" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&#160; We are developing rolapitant, niraparib, and TSR-011 on a global basis, and intend to develop any future product candidates globally, in order to more rapidly enroll patients and support regulatory submissions to health authorities outside of the United States.&#160; We believe that this will result in shortened development timelines, earlier submission of marketing authorization applications and, if the clinical results warrant, earlier regulatory approvals than would be expected if we were to conduct clinical development programs entirely within the United States.</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt 1.25in;text-indent:-.25in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt 1.25in;text-indent:-.25in;"><font size="2" face="Symbol" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&#183;</font><font size="1" style="font-size:3.0pt;">&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160; </font><i><font size="2" style="font-size:10.0pt;font-style:italic;">Commercial Operations in Key Markets.</font></i><font size="2" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&#160; We currently plan to commercialize our portfolio of cancer therapeutics and oncology supportive care products by deploying fully integrated sales and marketing organizations in core strategic markets&#151;specifically, North America, key European markets and China&#151;and establishing distributor networks or licensee arrangements in non-core markets around the world.&#160; We believe that building our own commercial operations function in our core strategic markets is an important component of our strategy because by doing so we expect to receive a greater return on our product investment than if we licensed these products to third parties for sale.&#160; Because our management team has experience commercializing products in these core strategic markets and understands the relevant sales, marketing and reimbursement frameworks, we expect to be able to generate higher revenues than if we were to sell exclusively through licensees or distributors.</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt 1.25in;text-indent:-.25in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><b><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;font-weight:bold;">Overview of the Market for Cancer Therapeutics and Oncology Supportive Care Products</font></b></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">Cancer is a group of diseases characterized by uncontrolled growth and spread of abnormal cells.&#160; The American Cancer Society estimated that in the United States in 2013, approximately 1.7&nbsp;million new cases of cancer would be diagnosed and more than 580,000 people would die from the disease.&#160; Current treatments for cancer include surgery, radiation therapy, chemotherapy, hormone therapy and targeted therapy.&#160; The IMS Institute for Healthcare Informatics estimated in a 2012 report that global oncology spending will exceed $90&nbsp;billion by 2017, representing the largest class of drug spending globally.&#160; The National Institutes of Health estimated in a 2011 analysis that direct medical costs (i.e., a total of all health expenditures) associated with cancer will reach $158 billion by 2020.</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">Many marketed products and product candidates for treating cancer patients that are currently being developed by biopharmaceutical companies are cytotoxic chemotherapies that exert their toxic effect on cancer generally through nonspecific damage to cellular components with the goal of causing cancer cell malfunction and cell death.&#160; Other products and product candidates alter cell metabolism or internal repair mechanisms leading to the demise of the cancer cell.&#160; More recently, targeted anti-cancer agents have been designed by scientists to inhibit the action of specific molecules within cancer cells that are driving the aberrant growth responsible for tumor development.&#160; Some of these targeted agents are developed in conjunction with companion diagnostic tests that are used by clinicians to determine if a patient&#146;s cancerous tumor contains these specific molecules and is, therefore, more likely to respond to a particular targeted therapy.&#160; For our current cancer therapeutics, we believe we have acquired product candidates where diagnostics or specific clinical criteria will allow us to identify cancer patients who will be more likely to respond to the therapeutic.&#160; In the future, our preference will be to in-license or acquire cancer therapeutics that can be developed in a targeted patient population enriched for those who will respond to the drug candidate.&#160; We expect that the characteristics of these compounds will permit us to design clinical trials that, if successful,</font></p>
<p align="center" style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-align:center;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p align="center" style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-align:center;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">8<a name="PB_8_162817_3020"></a></font></p>
<div style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><hr size="3" width="100%" noshade color="#010101" align="left"></div>
</div>
<!-- SEQ.=1,FOLIO='8',FILE='C:\JMS\skris\13-25458-1\task6623766\25458-1-bi.htm',USER='105340',CD='Mar 14 04:52 2014' -->

<br clear="all" style="page-break-before:always;">
<div>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;"><a href="#TableOfContents" title="Click to go to Table of Contents">Table of Contents</a></font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">will allow us to achieve clinical outcomes that will support regulatory approval for targeted patient groups and reimbursement by healthcare payors due to attractive risk/benefit metrics in the targeted population.</font></p>
<p align="center" style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-align:center;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">All of these approaches may be associated with various side effects experienced by cancer patients that result from the treatments having an adverse impact on normal functioning cells and organ systems.&#160; Some of the more common side effects of cancer therapy include nausea, vomiting or emesis, infections, fatigue and diarrhea.&#160; Supportive care products are frequently prescribed or administered to cancer patients to prevent or treat these side effects thereby allowing the patients to continue to receive potentially life prolonging cancer therapies.</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">Treatment centers (such as hospitals and community cancer centers) and the healthcare professionals who treat cancer patients (physicians, nurses, physician assistants and pharmacists) utilize various combinations of cancer therapeutics and oncology supportive care products to extend and improve the quality of life of these patients.&#160; Our strategy is aligned with these trends in cancer care; that is, to acquire, in-license and develop product candidates and to commercialize products that selectively treat cancers and those that address the side-effects from such treatments.</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><b><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;font-weight:bold;">Our Product Candidates</font></b></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">Our first three in-licensed product candidates are consistent with our strategy to develop and commercialize cancer therapeutics and oncology supportive care products.&#160; The following table summarizes the status of these product candidates.</font></p>
<p align="center" style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-align:center;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p align="center" style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-align:center;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman"><img border="0" width="608" height="356" src="g254581bii001.gif" alt="GRAPHIC"></font></p>
<p align="center" style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-align:center;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<div style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><hr size="1" width="25%" noshade color="black" align="left"></div>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt .5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">*in collaboration with SARC</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt .5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><b><i><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;font-style:italic;font-weight:bold;">Rolapitant&#151;Neurokinin-1 (NK-1) Receptor Antagonist</font></i></b></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><i><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;font-style:italic;">Overview</font></i></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">Rolapitant is a potent and long-acting NK-1 receptor antagonist that is being developed as a supportive care product for the prevention of CINV.&#160; We are investigating whether a single dose of rolapitant will, when administered along with the current standard of care for CINV (a 5-HT3 receptor antagonist plus a corticosteroid), significantly decrease vomiting and the use of rescue medication for nausea over the five-day period of risk for cancer patients receiving emetogenic chemotherapy</font></p>
<p align="center" style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-align:center;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p align="center" style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-align:center;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">9<a name="PB_9_163009_7748"></a></font></p>
<div style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><hr size="3" width="100%" noshade color="#010101" align="left"></div>
</div>
<!-- SEQ.=1,FOLIO='9',FILE='C:\JMS\skris\13-25458-1\task6623766\25458-1-bi.htm',USER='105340',CD='Mar 14 04:52 2014' -->

<br clear="all" style="page-break-before:always;">
<div>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;"><a href="#TableOfContents" title="Click to go to Table of Contents">Table of Contents</a></font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">as compared to the current standard of care alone.&#160; We obtained the exclusive worldwide rights to research, develop, manufacture, market and sell rolapitant from OPKO in December&nbsp;2010.</font></p>
<p align="center" style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-align:center;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><i><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;font-style:italic;">Chemotherapy Induced Nausea and Vomiting</font></i></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">According to CINV prevention and treatment guidelines developed and published by respected oncology organizations such as the National Cancer Care Network, or NCCN, Multinational Association for Supportive Care in Cancer, or MASCC, and American Society of Clinical Oncology, or ASCO, if not prevented by prophylaxis, CINV has the potential to afflict up to 90% or more of cancer patients undergoing chemotherapy, depending upon the type of chemotherapy administered, the dosing schedule of the chemotherapy and the patients&#146; gender, among other predisposing factors.&#160; Prolonged nausea and vomiting may result in unwanted weight loss, dehydration and malnutrition as well as hospitalization.&#160; If not prevented, CINV may result in a delay or discontinuation of chemotherapy treatment.&#160; Based on our analysis of market data provided by IMS Health Incorporated and patient treatment data collected by Ipsos Healthcare, a market research firm, we estimate that the NK-1 receptor antagonist market consists of approximately 5&nbsp;million potential treatments administered on the first day of chemotherapy in combination with the current standard of care for the prevention of CINV.&#160; We estimate that approximately 85-90% of these potential 5 million treatments would be for patients receiving the combination of anthracycline and cyclophosphamide, or AC, based chemotherapy, for the treatment of breast cancer, or for certain patients receiving moderately emetogenic chemotherapy, or MEC, for other types of cancer.&#160; The remaining potential market treatments are for patients receiving highly emetogenic chemotherapy, or HEC, regimens that contain cisplatin.&#160; Current treatment guidelines recommend that all cancer patients receiving AC based chemotherapy for breast cancer and cisplatin containing regimens should be treated with an NK-1 receptor antagonist in addition to the current standard of care for CINV, while cancer patients receiving MEC regimens could in appropriate circumstances be treated with an NK-1 receptor antagonist in addition to the current standard of care for the prevention of CINV.&#160; The NCCN guidelines clarify that it is appropriate to treat cancer patients receiving a MEC regimen that utilizes carboplatin with an NK-1 receptor antagonist in addition to the current standard of care for CINV.</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">The Journal of Supportive Oncology has reported that the perception of healthcare professionals as to whether their patients experience delayed nausea and vomiting due to chemotherapy differs significantly from actual patient experience.&#160; Specifically, this journal reported in 2004 that healthcare professionals perceived that 76% of their patients did not experience delayed nausea and 91% of their patients did not experience delayed vomiting when actual patient experience indicated that only 43% of patients did not experience delayed nausea and 59% of patients did not experience delayed vomiting.</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">The current standard of care for CINV consists of a 5-HT3 receptor antagonist (usually one of ondansetron, granisetron, dolasetron or palonosetron) plus a corticosteroid (usually dexamethasone).&#160; 5-HT3, or serotonin sub-type&nbsp;3, receptor antagonists block the binding of serotonin to the 5-HT3 receptor in specific nerve endings in the body and the brain, resulting in a reduction in nausea and vomiting in patients at risk for CINV.&#160; Optimal protection against CINV is provided to certain patients when an NK-1 receptor antagonist is administered together with a 5-HT3 receptor antagonist.&#160; NK-1 receptor antagonists block substance P from binding to NK-1 receptors.&#160; Substance P is a natural substance in the brain that binds to the NK-1 receptor and represents a second mechanism that induces nausea and vomiting.&#160; Currently, oral aprepitant and its IV pro-drug fosaprepitant, which are both known by the brand name EMEND and marketed by Merck, are the only commercially available NK-1 receptor antagonists.&#160; Based upon Merck&#146;s announcement of its financial results for the year ended December&nbsp;31, 2013, EMEND generated $507&nbsp;million (unaudited) in global revenues in 2013.&#160; We believe there is a significant need for another NK-1 receptor antagonist with improved properties over EMEND.</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">The following chart summarizes rankings of chemotherapy treatment side effects from a study of patients diagnosed with ovarian, primary peritoneal or fallopian tube cancer who received at least three cycles of platinum-based chemotherapy.&#160; Using a visual analog score, where patients rank different side effects on a scale from zero to 100, with zero being the least favorable and 100 being the most favorable, patients evaluated different side effects related to cancer and chemotherapy treatment, including CINV 1-6, representing different scenarios of CINV.&#160; According to the study, the most favorable side effects included perfect health and clinical remission, followed closely by CINV 1, or complete-to-almost-complete control of CINV.&#160; By contrast, all of the least favorable side effects, other than death, included nausea and vomiting.&#160; This study, the results of which are summarized in the figure below, shows that patients view CINV as one of the least favorable side effects of chemotherapy treatment.</font></p>
<p align="center" style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-align:center;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p align="center" style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-align:center;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">10<a name="PB_10_163051_141"></a></font></p>
<div style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><hr size="3" width="100%" noshade color="#010101" align="left"></div>
</div>
<!-- SEQ.=1,FOLIO='10',FILE='C:\JMS\skris\13-25458-1\task6623766\25458-1-bi.htm',USER='105340',CD='Mar 14 04:52 2014' -->

<br clear="all" style="page-break-before:always;">
<div>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;"><a href="#TableOfContents" title="Click to go to Table of Contents">Table of Contents</a></font></p>
<p align="center" style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-align:center;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p align="center" style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-align:center;"><b><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;font-weight:bold;">Patient Rankings of Chemotherapy Treatment Side Effects</font></b></p>
<p align="center" style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-align:center;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p align="center" style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-align:center;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman"><img border="0" width="608" height="301" src="g254581bii002.jpg"></font></p>
<p align="center" style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-align:center;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">Source:&#160; Adapted from Charlotte&nbsp;C. Sun et al., <i>Rankings and symptom assessments of side effects from chemotherapy: insights from experienced patients with ovarian cancer,</i> Support Care Cancer (2005).</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">There are two phases associated with CINV: acute and delayed.&#160; The acute phase occurs within the first 24&nbsp;hours following chemotherapy treatment.&#160; It is believed that this phase is caused largely by chemotherapy-induced increases in serotonin release and activation of 5-HT3 receptors on vagal afferent neurons in the gut.</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">There are currently four 5-HT3 receptor antagonists on the market in the United States (ondansetron, palonosetron, granisetron and dolasetron) and one additional agent that is available in several international markets (tropisetron), all of which are clinically effective in preventing acute CINV, particularly when given in combination with a corticosteroid, such as dexamethasone.&#160; Despite the success of the 5-HT3 receptor antagonists, protection of patients from acute CINV is not complete because other neurotransmitters are also involved in the onset of CINV.&#160; Furthermore, there remains a strong need to develop potent therapies effective to prevent or treat delayed CINV.</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">Delayed CINV is described as occurring after 24&nbsp;hours and up to five days following emetogenic chemotherapy and is believed to be primarily driven by a different etiology than acute CINV.&#160; Combination therapy with a corticosteroid and 5-HT3 receptor antagonist, particularly with the first generation 5-HT3 receptor antagonists, is less effective during the delayed phase than it is in the acute phase of CINV.&#160; This is because the primary etiology of delayed CINV appears to involve substance P.&#160; Substance P binds to NK-1 receptors, which are highly concentrated in the brain.&#160; Activation of NK-1 receptors in the brain plays a central role in nausea and vomiting induced by emetogenic stimuli, including certain cancer chemotherapies.&#160; An NK-1 receptor antagonist works by blocking the binding of substance P with NK-1 receptors.&#160; A clinical study that employed positron emission tomography, a medical technique utilized for imaging biochemical activity within the body, demonstrated that rolapitant, provided in single oral doses ranging from 5mg to 200mg, binds to brain NK-1 receptors.&#160; At a time point of five days following administration of a single 200mg dose, over 90% of NK-1 receptors remained occupied by rolapitant.&#160; The addition of an NK-1 receptor antagonist to the standard of care (a 5-HT3 receptor antagonist plus a corticosteroid) has been demonstrated to improve the management of both acute and delayed CINV that is experienced by cancer patients undergoing chemotherapy.</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">Despite the importance of the NK-1 receptor in the etiology of both acute and delayed emesis, there are only two approved products that target this receptor, oral aprepitant and its IV pro-drug fosaprepitant, which are both known by the brand name EMEND and marketed by Merck.&#160; In multiple clinical trials, EMEND provided significantly better protection against both acute and delayed emesis when it was added to a 5-HT3 receptor antagonist and corticosteroid as compared to the 5-HT3 receptor antagonist and corticosteroid alone.&#160; EMEND was initially introduced as an oral formulation in 2003.&#160; In</font></p>
<p align="center" style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-align:center;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p align="center" style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-align:center;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">11<a name="PB_11_163146_7608"></a></font></p>
<div style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><hr size="3" width="100%" noshade color="#010101" align="left"></div>
</div>
<!-- SEQ.=1,FOLIO='11',FILE='C:\JMS\skris\13-25458-1\task6623766\25458-1-bi.htm',USER='105340',CD='Mar 14 04:52 2014' -->

<br clear="all" style="page-break-before:always;">
<div style="font-family:Times New Roman;">
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;"><a href="#TableOfContents" title="Click to go to Table of Contents">Table of Contents</a></font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">2010, Merck introduced a single-dose IV formulation of EMEND, which we believe currently accounts for approximately 80% of all EMEND usage.</font></p>
<p align="center" style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-align:center;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">We believe that the product profile of rolapitant will provide a long-lasting clinical benefit to patients with a better drug interaction profile than the other NK-1 receptor antagonists on the market or in development.&#160; Recent pharmacokinetic and pharmacodynamic studies demonstrate that rolapitant has a half-life of approximately 180&nbsp;hours and has high affinity for the human NK-1 receptor (Ki =0.7nM).&#160; Positron emission tomography (or PET) scans of patients five&nbsp;days after a single oral 200mg dose of rolapitant demonstrate greater than 90% NK-1 receptor occupancy.&#160; Further, data from clinical studies demonstrate that rolapitant is not an inhibitor or inducer of cytochrome P450 3A4 isoenzyme, or CYP3A4.&#160; When a drug inhibits or induces CYP3A4, it can lead to an adverse effect on the ability to metabolize other drugs.&#160; The data indicates that rolapitant does not alter the pharmacokinetics of midazolam or other tested CYP3A4 substrates, and consequently is unlikely to have an effect on the pharmacokinetics of drugs metabolized by CYP3A4.&#160; Based upon this data, and in contrast with the current oral and IV NK-1 receptor antagonist on the market and another oral NK-1 receptor antagonist in clinical development, we believe that administration of rolapitant is unlikely to cause a clinically significant pharmacokinetic interaction with many commonly used drugs intended for cancer patients undergoing chemotherapy.&#160; Additionally, when compared to these other NK-1 receptor antagonists using their respective registration studies, rolapitant demonstrated a greater complete response (that is, no emesis and no use of rescue medication compared to control) and greater complete protection compared to control (that is, a complete response and no significant nausea) during the delayed and overall period following chemotherapy (24&nbsp;to&nbsp;120 hours and 0&nbsp;to&nbsp;120 hours, respectively).</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><i><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;font-style:italic;">Clinical Guidelines for the Usage of 5-HT3 and NK-1 Receptor Antagonists</font></i></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">Most patients who receive preventative therapy for CINV receive chemotherapy regimens that are defined as having either high or moderate risk.&#160; HEC regimens include those containing cisplatin, and for regulatory approval of drugs to prevent CINV in patients receiving HEC, this is an important patient population to study.&#160; CINV prevention and treatment guidelines developed and published by respected oncology organizations such as NCCN, MASCC, and ASCO also define anthracycline-cyclophosphamide containing treatment regimens as HEC.&#160; Such regimens are frequently utilized to treat certain types of breast cancer.&#160; MEC is categorized by ASCO, NCCN and other treatment guidelines, and includes chemotherapy agents such as carboplatin, irinotecan, ifosfamide and cisplatin when administered in doses of less than 50mg/m</font><font size="1" style="font-size:6.5pt;position:relative;top:-3.0pt;">2</font><font size="2" style="font-size:10.0pt;">.</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<table border="0" cellspacing="0" cellpadding="0" width="73%" style="border-collapse:collapse;margin-left:1.0in;width:73.34%;">
<tr>
<td width="50%" valign="bottom" style="border:none;border-bottom:solid windowtext 1.0pt;padding:0in 0in 0in 0in;width:50.34%;">
<p align="center" style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-align:center;"><b><font size="1" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:8.0pt;font-weight:bold;">Emetogenic&nbsp;Potential&nbsp;(ASCO,<br>   MASCC,&nbsp;NCCN&nbsp;Guidelines)</font></b></p>    </td>
<td width="3%" valign="bottom" style="padding:0in 0in 0in 0in;width:3.64%;">
<p align="center" style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-align:center;"><b><font size="1" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:1.0pt;font-weight:bold;">&nbsp;</font></b></p>    </td>
<td width="46%" valign="bottom" style="border:none;border-bottom:solid windowtext 1.0pt;padding:0in 0in 0in 0in;width:46.02%;">
<p align="center" style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-align:center;"><b><font size="1" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:8.0pt;font-weight:bold;">Proportion&nbsp;of&nbsp;Patients&nbsp;Who&nbsp;Will<br>   Experience<br>   Emesis&nbsp;in&nbsp;the&nbsp;Absence&nbsp;of<br>   Effective&nbsp;Antiemetic&nbsp;Prophylaxis</font></b></p>    </td>   </tr>
<tr>
<td width="50%" valign="top" bgcolor="#CCEEFF" style="background:#CCEEFF;border:none;padding:0in 0in 0in 0in;width:50.34%;">
<p align="center" style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-align:center;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">High</font></p>    </td>
<td width="3%" valign="bottom" bgcolor="#CCEEFF" style="background:#CCEEFF;padding:0in 0in 0in 0in;width:3.64%;">
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:1.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>    </td>
<td width="46%" valign="top" bgcolor="#CCEEFF" style="background:#CCEEFF;border:none;padding:0in 0in 0in 0in;width:46.02%;">
<p align="center" style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-align:center;"><u><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&gt;</font></u><font size="2" style="font-size:10.0pt;">90%   of patients</font></p>    </td>   </tr>
<tr>
<td width="50%" valign="top" style="padding:0in 0in 0in 0in;width:50.34%;">
<p align="center" style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-align:center;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">Moderate</font></p>    </td>
<td width="3%" valign="bottom" style="padding:0in 0in 0in 0in;width:3.64%;">
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:1.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>    </td>
<td width="46%" valign="top" style="padding:0in 0in 0in 0in;width:46.02%;">
<p align="center" style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-align:center;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">30 - 90% of patients</font></p>    </td>   </tr>
<tr>
<td width="50%" valign="top" bgcolor="#CCEEFF" style="background:#CCEEFF;padding:0in 0in 0in 0in;width:50.34%;">
<p align="center" style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-align:center;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">Low</font></p>    </td>
<td width="3%" valign="bottom" bgcolor="#CCEEFF" style="background:#CCEEFF;padding:0in 0in 0in 0in;width:3.64%;">
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:1.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>    </td>
<td width="46%" valign="top" bgcolor="#CCEEFF" style="background:#CCEEFF;padding:0in 0in 0in 0in;width:46.02%;">
<p align="center" style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-align:center;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">10 - 30% of patients</font></p>    </td>   </tr>
<tr>
<td width="50%" valign="top" style="padding:0in 0in 0in 0in;width:50.34%;">
<p align="center" style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-align:center;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">Minimal</font></p>    </td>
<td width="3%" valign="bottom" style="padding:0in 0in 0in 0in;width:3.64%;">
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:1.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>    </td>
<td width="46%" valign="top" style="padding:0in 0in 0in 0in;width:46.02%;">
<p align="center" style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-align:center;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&lt;10% of patients</font></p>    </td>   </tr>  </table>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">According to current treatment guidelines, the risk of vomiting for patients receiving HEC regimens, including anthracycline-cyclophosphamide, is equal to or greater than 90%.&#160; The current treatment guidelines also suggest that MEC regimens are associated with a risk of vomiting in the range of 30% to 90%.&#160; Based on our analysis of the market data and patient treatment data described above, we estimate that patients receiving HEC regimens, including for these purposes AC based chemotherapy, make up approximately 70% of the potential NK-1 receptor antagonist treatment market and patients receiving MEC regimens make up approximately 30% of the market.</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">While the United States NK-1 market was approximately $290&nbsp;million in 2013 (represented by oral and IV formulations of EMEND), we believe there is a larger market opportunity for the class.&#160; Our analysis of data from IMS Health indicates that following the 2011 launch of a single dose,&nbsp;IV-only regimen (versus the pre-existing three-day oral regimen), for the aprepitant IV pro-drug, fosaprepitant, the number of EMEND patient treatments grew over 20% for two consecutive years.&#160; We believe the market will expand further based upon the combined sales and marketing activities and enhanced educational initiatives associated with three companies marketing NK-1 receptor antagonists.&#160; In addition to our activities and initiatives and those of Merck, we expect that Helsinn Healthcare and Eisai&nbsp;Inc. will be introducing a combination NK-1 receptor antagonist and 5-HT3 receptor antagonist product, netupitant plus Aloxi (palonosetron HCl).&#160; Overall trends in the market, growing awareness of supportive care issues and the implementation of guidelines for patient</font></p>
<p align="center" style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-align:center;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p align="center" style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-align:center;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">12<a name="PB_12_163430_8146"></a></font></p>
<div style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><hr size="3" width="100%" noshade color="#010101" align="left"></div>
</div>
<!-- SEQ.=1,FOLIO='12',FILE='C:\JMS\skris\13-25458-1\task6623766\25458-1-bi.htm',USER='105340',CD='Mar 14 04:52 2014' -->

<br clear="all" style="page-break-before:always;">
<div style="font-family:Times New Roman;">
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;"><a href="#TableOfContents" title="Click to go to Table of Contents">Table of Contents</a></font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">care, including the prevention of CINV, that are developed and published by oncology organizations, may also lead to greater use of NK-1 receptor antagonists.</font></p>
<p align="center" style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-align:center;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><i><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;font-style:italic;">Key Characteristics of Rolapitant</font></i></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">Rolapitant is a highly potent, long acting NK-1 receptor antagonist that, if approved, may provide control of CINV over the five-day period of risk for cancer patients receiving emetogenic chemotherapy, including HEC and MEC.&#160; The safety and pharmacokinetic profile observed in Phase&nbsp;1 clinical trials, combined with the clinical activity and safety profile observed in a randomized Phase&nbsp;2 clinical trial described below, suggest that rolapitant represents a potential advance in the prevention of CINV.&#160; We believe that rolapitant has several important characteristics, including:</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt .75in;text-indent:-.25in;"><font size="2" face="Symbol" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&#183;</font><font size="1" style="font-size:3.0pt;">&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160; </font><i><font size="2" style="font-size:10.0pt;font-style:italic;">Long Half-Life.</font></i><font size="2" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&#160; Recent pharmacokinetic and pharmacodynamic studies demonstrate that rolapitant has a half-life of approximately 180&nbsp;hours and has high affinity for the human NK-1 receptor (Ki =0.7nM).&#160; Positron emission tomography (or PET) scans of patients five&nbsp;days after a single oral 200mg dose of rolapitant demonstrate greater than 90% NK-1 receptor occupancy.</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt .75in;text-indent:-.25in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt .75in;text-indent:-.25in;"><font size="2" face="Symbol" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&#183;</font><font size="1" style="font-size:3.0pt;">&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160; </font><i><font size="2" style="font-size:10.0pt;font-style:italic;">Reduced Risk of Drug Interactions.</font></i><font size="2" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&#160; Data from clinical studies demonstrate that rolapitant is not an inhibitor or inducer of cytochrome P450 3A4 isoenzyme, or CYP3A4.&#160; CYP3A4 is a liver enzyme that is responsible for the metabolism of a number of drugs.&#160; When a drug inhibits or induces CYP3A4, it can lead to an adverse effect on the ability to metabolize other drugs.&#160; The data indicates that rolapitant does not alter the pharmacokinetics of midazolam or other tested CYP3A4 substrates, and consequently is unlikely to have an effect on the pharmacokinetics of drugs metabolized by CYP3A4.&#160; Based upon this data, and in contrast with the current oral and IV NK-1 receptor antagonist on the market </font><font size="2" style="font-size:10.0pt;">and another oral NK-1 receptor antagonist in clinical development</font><font size="2" style="font-size:10.0pt;">, we believe that administration of rolapitant is unlikely to cause a clinically significant pharmacokinetic interaction with many commonly used drugs intended for cancer patients undergoing chemotherapy.</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt .75in;text-indent:-.25in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt .75in;text-indent:-.25in;"><font size="2" face="Symbol" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&#183;</font><font size="1" style="font-size:3.0pt;">&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160; </font><i><font size="2" style="font-size:10.0pt;font-style:italic;">Rapid Onset of Activity.</font></i><font size="2" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&#160; In a Phase&nbsp;2 clinical trial, time to first emesis or use of rescue medication for rolapitant versus the control group showed rapid onset of activity within approximately three hours of completing chemotherapy treatment, which represents a quicker onset of action than has been observed in other agents in its class.</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt .75in;text-indent:-.25in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><i><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;font-style:italic;">Rolapitant Clinical Development</font></i></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">In 2008, Schering-Plough completed three Phase&nbsp;2 clinical trials in which rolapitant was evaluated for the prevention of CINV, PONV and the treatment of chronic cough.&#160; One of these trials was designed to assess the efficacy and safety of rolapitant for the prevention of CINV for up to six cycles of chemotherapy, and to determine a Phase&nbsp;3 dose.&#160; This was a multicenter, randomized, double-blind clinical trial in which 454 cancer patients receiving HEC were administered a 5-HT3 receptor antagonist and a corticosteroid (ondansetron and dexamethasone), and randomized in equal fashion to groups receiving either placebo or 10mg, 25mg, 100mg or 200mg of a single dose oral formulation of rolapitant.&#160; Subjects recorded episodes of emesis, severity of nausea, and use of rescue medications daily in a subject diary from days one through six of cycle 1.</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">The rolapitant 200mg group, compared to the control group, had significantly greater complete response rates, meaning no emesis and no use of rescue medication, in the overall phase, meaning zero to 120&nbsp;hours after receipt of HEC, the acute phase, meaning zero to 24&nbsp;hours after receipt of HEC, and the delayed phase, meaning greater than 24&nbsp;hours to 120&nbsp;hours after receipt of HEC.&#160; The tables below present the comparisons of complete response rates in the rolapitant groups and the control group for the overall, acute and delayed phases.</font></p>
<p align="center" style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-align:center;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p align="center" style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-align:center;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">13<a name="PB_13_163458_7091"></a></font></p>
<div style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><hr size="3" width="100%" noshade color="#010101" align="left"></div>
</div>

<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">%</font></p>    </td>
<td width="14%" valign="bottom" bgcolor="#CCEEFF" style="background:#CCEEFF;padding:0in 0in 0in 0in;width:14.32%;">
<p align="right" style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-align:right;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">0.788</font></p>    </td>
<td width="1%" valign="bottom" bgcolor="#CCEEFF" style="background:#CCEEFF;padding:0in 0in 0in 0in;width:1.12%;">
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:1.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>    </td>   </tr>  </table>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">In clinical trials, the p-value is a measure of how strongly the data support a real difference between the effects of treatment and control. The smaller the p-value, the stronger the evidence. Conventionally, if the p-value is less than 0.05, the presumption is that there is a real difference between the treatment and control groups, and the results are deemed statistically significant. Rates for no significant nausea for the 200mg rolapitant dose group also demonstrated a superior treatment effect versus the control group in the overall, acute, and delayed phases of CINV. A validated questionnaire was used to assess patient quality of life and these data also demonstrated statistically significant better quality of life scores for the treatment group versus the control group. The table below presents the comparisons of rates for no significant nausea in the 200mg rolapitant dose group and the control group for the overall, acute and delayed phases.</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>

<p align="right" style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-align:right;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">48</font></p>    </td>
<td width="2%" valign="bottom" bgcolor="#CCEEFF" style="background:#CCEEFF;padding:0in 0in 0in 0in;width:2.72%;">
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">%</font></p>    </td>
<td width="14%" valign="bottom" bgcolor="#CCEEFF" style="background:#CCEEFF;padding:0in 0in 0in 0in;width:14.12%;">
<p align="right" style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-align:right;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">0.026</font></p>    </td>
<td width="1%" valign="bottom" bgcolor="#CCEEFF" style="background:#CCEEFF;padding:0in 0in 0in 0in;width:1.1%;">
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:1.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>    </td>   </tr>  </table>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">Treatment-related adverse events were mild and included constipation, headache, fatigue and dizziness.&#160; Overall, serious adverse events occurred with similar incidences across all treatment groups (9% to 14%).&#160; The most common serious adverse events were neutropenia (a disorder characterized by an abnormally low number of certain types of white blood cells), febrile neutropenia (the development of fever, often with signs of infection, in a patient with neutropenia), vomiting, dehydration, nausea and pneumonia.&#160; These events, however, were considered by investigators to be related to chemotherapy or the underlying cancer and not to rolapitant.&#160; Data from this clinical study demonstrated that a dose of 200mg rolapitant administered with a 5-HT3 receptor antagonist and dexamethasone achieved statistically significant improvement in preventing CINV than 5-HT3 receptor antagonist and dexamethasone alone, and this dose was selected for advancement into Phase&nbsp;3 clinical trials.</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">Results from the Phase&nbsp;2 clinical trial discussed above demonstrated what we believe is a promising level of activity for CINV prevention for the five-day period following administration of chemotherapy, the period during which patients are at highest risk for CINV.&#160; We presented data from the Phase&nbsp;2 clinical trial of rolapitant for the prevention of CINV at the ASCO annual meeting in June&nbsp;2012.</font></p>
<p align="center" style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-align:center;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p align="center" style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-align:center;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">14<a name="PB_14_103352_5796"></a></font></p>
<div style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><hr size="3" width="100%" noshade color="#010101" align="left"></div>
</div>
<!-- SEQ.=1,FOLIO='14',FILE='C:\JMS\108199\13-25458-1\task6623666\25458-1-bk.htm',USER='108199',CD='Mar 14 04:20 2014' -->

<br clear="all" style="page-break-before:always;">
<div style="font-family:Times New Roman;">
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;"><a href="#TableOfContents" title="Click to go to Table of Contents">Table of Contents</a></font></p>
<p align="center" style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-align:center;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><i><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;font-style:italic;">Rolapitant Phase&nbsp;3 Clinical Program</font></i></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">Based on the results of the Phase&nbsp;2 clinical trial, in early 2012, we enrolled the first patient in our Phase&nbsp;3 clinical program for oral rolapitant.&#160; The Phase&nbsp;3 clinical program consists of approximately 2,400 patients participating in one of three Phase&nbsp;3 clinical trials focused on evaluating rolapitant plus the standard of care compared with control plus the standard of care.&#160; This global program consists of two randomized, double-blind, active-controlled clinical trials evaluating the efficacy of a single 200mg oral dose of rolapitant in patients receiving HEC, and one clinical trial evaluating the efficacy of a single 200mg oral dose of rolapitant in patients receiving MEC.&#160; In each of the Phase&nbsp;3 clinical trials the standard of care consists of the 5-HT3 receptor antagonist granisetron in combination with the corticosteroid dexamethasone.&#160; The patients in these clinical trials are being evaluated for evidence of an improvement in control of nausea and vomiting during the acute, delayed and overall periods between zero and 120&nbsp;hours post administration of chemotherapy.&#160; The primary outcome of each trial is based on complete response (defined as no emesis and no use of rescue medication) in the delayed phase (24&nbsp;hours to 120&nbsp;hours).&#160; Additional outcome measures include complete response for other time points, the incidence and intensity of nausea, and safety and tolerability.</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">In December&nbsp;2013, we announced </font><font size="2" style="font-size:10.0pt;">top-line results of two completed Phase 3 trials of rolapitant.&#160; The first Phase 3 study of rolapitant was an international, multicenter, randomized, double-blind, active-controlled study that enrolled 1,369 cancer patients receiving MEC, approximately half of whom were receiving anthracycline-based treatment for breast cancer.&#160; Patients were randomized to receive either control, which consisted of a 5-HT3&nbsp;receptor antagonist plus dexamethasone, or 200mg of oral rolapitant plus control.&#160; The rolapitant arm successfully achieved statistical significance over the control arm for the primary endpoint of complete response in the delayed phase.&#160; </font><font size="2" style="font-size:10.0pt;">Secondary endpoints were tested in a hierarchical manner and multiplicity was controlled using the Bonferroni-Holm method.&#160; </font><font size="2" style="font-size:10.0pt;">A greater proportion of patients treated with rolapitant in this trial achieved a complete response in the acute and overall phases and experienced no significant nausea compared to the control arm, although statistical significance was not met for these secondary endpoints when tested in this manner.</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">The second Phase 3 study of rolapitant was an international, multicenter, randomized, double-blind, active-controlled study that enrolled 555 patients receiving HEC, defined as regimens which contain cisplatin at a dose equal to or greater than 60 mg/m</font><font size="1" style="font-size:6.5pt;position:relative;top:-3.0pt;">2</font><font size="2" style="font-size:10.0pt;">.&#160; Patients were randomized to receive either control, which consisted of 5-HT3&nbsp;receptor antagonist plus dexamethasone, or 200mg of oral rolapitant plus control.&#160; Similar to the MEC study, the rolapitant arm in the HEC study successfully achieved statistical significance over the control arm for the primary endpoint of complete response in the delayed phase of CINV.&#160; </font><font size="2" style="font-size:10.0pt;">Again, secondary endpoints were tested in a hierarchical manner and multiplicity was controlled using the Bonferroni-Holm method.&#160; </font><font size="2" style="font-size:10.0pt;">A greater proportion of patients treated with rolapitant in this trial achieved a complete response in the acute and overall phases and experienced no significant nausea compared to the control arm, although statistical significance was not met for these secondary endpoints.</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">In each Phase 3 study, safety and tolerability data for patients who received rolapitant were similar to the results for those who received control, and were consistent with earlier clinical studies. The most frequently observed adverse events were balanced across treatment arms and included fatigue, alopecia and loss of appetite.</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">We continue to evaluate patients receiving HEC in a third and final Phase 3 trial of rolapitant, which has completed enrollment, and we expect to file an NDA submission for oral rolapitant in mid-2014.&#160; Full analyses of data from all three rolapitant pivotal Phase 3 trials have been submitted for presentation at a future medical meeting.</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><i><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;font-style:italic;">Intravenous Formulation of Rolapitant</font></i></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">We are developing a single dose rolapitant IV formulation to address what we believe is the market need for this dosage form.&#160; We believe this formulation will provide physicians with an additional route of administering rolapitant, while also alleviating certain concerns associated with payor pre-approval, logistics and pharmacy availability that are sometimes associated with oral formulations of drugs utilized by cancer patients.&#160; </font><font size="2" style="font-size:10.0pt;">In January&nbsp;2014, in a single ascending dose study we identified a dose of our IV formulation of rolapitant that we believe is likely to achieve bioequivalence to a 200mg dose of oral rolapitant</font><font size="2" style="font-size:10.0pt;">.&#160; We may conduct a bridging safety study in patients to support regulatory approval of the IV formulation.&#160; Current plans for the development of the IV formulation are dependent on the success of the oral formulation.&#160; We expect that the NDA that we submit to the FDA for the IV formulation of rolapitant will rely heavily on, and reference data in, our NDA submission for oral rolapitant.&#160; We plan to discuss this approach with the FDA prior to conducting the bioequivalence</font></p>
<p align="center" style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-align:center;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p align="center" style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-align:center;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">15<a name="PB_15_103352_2897"></a></font></p>
<div style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><hr size="3" width="100%" noshade color="#010101" align="left"></div>
</div>
