<br clear="all" style="page-break-before:always;">
<div style="font-family:Times New Roman;">
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;"><a href="#TableOfContents" title="Click to go to Table of Contents">Table of Contents</a></font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">or bridging safety study.&#160; We expect to launch an IV formulation of rolapitant approximately one year following the launch of the oral formulation.</font></p>
<p align="center" style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-align:center;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><b><i><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;font-style:italic;font-weight:bold;">Niraparib&#151;Poly (ADP-ribose) Polymerase (PARP) Inhibitor</font></i></b></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><i><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;font-style:italic;">Overview</font></i></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">Niraparib, formerly known as MK-4827, is an orally active and potent poly (ADP-ribose) polymerase, or PARP, inhibitor.&#160; Niraparib has demonstrated promising results in a Phase&nbsp;1 clinical trial in advanced cancer patients.&#160; In the trial, a maximum tolerated dose of 300mg of niraparib was determined, and anti-tumor activity in BRCA-deficient cancers was also observed.&#160; BRCA1 and BRCA2 belong to a class of human genes, the mutation of which has been linked to certain types of cancers, including breast, ovarian and lung.&#160; In July&nbsp;2013, we dosed the first patient in a Phase 3 clinical trial evaluating niraparib for the treatment of patients with high grade serous, </font><font size="2" style="font-size:10.0pt;">platinum sensitive, relapsed ovarian cancer.&#160; We also plan to evaluate niraparib in breast cancer patients with germline BRCA mutations in a Phase 3 clinical trial, in which we expect to dose the first patient in the first quarter of 2014.&#160; We also are collaborating with SARC to evaluate niraparib in combination with temozolomide for the treatment of Ewing&#146;s sarcoma.&#160; We may also evaluate niraparib for the treatment of gastric, lung, and prostate cancer, and we plan to evaluate niraparib in combination preclinical pharmacology studies with our immuno-oncology anti-tumor agents.</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">PARP is a family of proteins involved in many functions in a cell, including DNA repair, gene expression, cell cycle control, intracellular trafficking and energy metabolism.&#160; PARP inhibitors have shown preclinical efficacy as a monotherapy against tumors with existing defects, such as BRCA1 and BRCA2, by compromising their ability to repair DNA, and as a combination therapy when administered together with anti-cancer agents that induce DNA damage.&#160; Results to date for clinical trials of PARP inhibitors indicate anti-cancer activity, which is particularly noteworthy in patients with germline BRCA mutations.</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">In October&nbsp;2012, we entered into two license agreements with AstraZeneca UK Limited, having aggregate upfront payments of $0.4 million.&#160; These agreements provide us with the exclusive right to certain methods of treating patients with PARP inhibitors solely with respect to niraparib.&#160; Under certain circumstances, we may be required to make milestone and royalty payments to AstraZeneca UK Limited based on the achievement of certain development and regulatory milestone events with regard to niraparib, and on net sales of niraparib.&#160; </font><font size="2" style="font-size:10.0pt;">We made milestone payments totaling $0.2 million to AstraZeneca during the year ended December&nbsp;31, 2013.</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><i><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;font-style:italic;">Background on PARP Inhibitors</font></i></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">As mentioned above, PARP is a family of proteins involved in many functions in a cell.&#160; One well studied area of PARP activity relates to DNA repair.</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">DNA contains genetic instructions used in the development and functioning of most known living organisms.&#160; DNA can be damaged by many sorts of mutagens, including oxidizing agents, alkylating agents, ultraviolet light and X-rays.&#160; An important property of DNA is that it can replicate, or make copies of itself.&#160; This is critical when cells divide because each new cell needs to have an exact copy of the DNA present in the old cell.&#160; It is also critical to the integrity and survival of cells that DNA damage can be repaired.&#160; Cells have evolved multiple mechanisms to enable such DNA repair, and these mechanisms are complementary to each other, each driving repair of specific types of DNA damage.&#160; If a cell&#146;s DNA damage repair system is overpowered, then the cell is programmed to die.</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">Radiation and certain chemotherapies such as alkylating agents and topoisomerase inhibitors induce significant damage to tumor cells, which results in programmed cell death.&#160; DNA repair mechanisms may reduce the activity of these anti-cancer therapies but, conversely, inhibition of DNA repair processes may enhance the effects of DNA-damaging anti-cancer therapy.&#160; PARP is essential for some DNA repair processes and therefore may be an important target in cancer therapy.&#160; PARP inhibitors have shown preclinical efficacy as monotherapy against tumors with existing defects, such as BRCA1 and BRCA2, that compromise their ability to repair DNA, and as a combination therapy when administered together with anti-cancer agents that induce DNA damage.</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">Clinical trial results to date suggest that PARP inhibitors may be effective as a monotherapy in cancer patients with mutations in genes such as BRCA1 and BRCA2.&#160; PARP inhibitors have also been explored in numerous clinical trials as</font></p>
<p align="center" style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-align:center;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p align="center" style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-align:center;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">16<a name="PB_16_103352_3020"></a></font></p>
<div style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><hr size="3" width="100%" noshade color="#010101" align="left"></div>
</div>
<!-- SEQ.=1,FOLIO='16',FILE='C:\JMS\108199\13-25458-1\task6623666\25458-1-bk.htm',USER='108199',CD='Mar 14 04:20 2014' -->

<br clear="all" style="page-break-before:always;">
<div style="font-family:Times New Roman;">
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;"><a href="#TableOfContents" title="Click to go to Table of Contents">Table of Contents</a></font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">potentiators of chemotherapy, including in combination with temozolomide, cisplatin, carboplatin, gemcitabine and topotecan.</font></p>
<p align="center" style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-align:center;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><i><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;font-style:italic;">Key Characteristics of Niraparib</font></i></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">Niraparib is an investigational, orally active and potent PARP inhibitor that we believe has certain desirable characteristics.&#160; Based upon our review of the data, we believe that niraparib has the potential to inhibit growth of solid tumors in cancer patients.&#160; The nonclinical and Phase&nbsp;1 clinical data show that niraparib may have advantages as a treatment for certain cancers, including:</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt .75in;text-indent:-.25in;"><font size="2" face="Symbol" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&#183;</font><font size="1" style="font-size:3.0pt;">&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160; </font><font size="2" style="font-size:10.0pt;">potent inhibition of PARP and demonstrated tumor growth inhibition in tumor models;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt .75in;text-indent:-.25in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt .75in;text-indent:-.25in;"><font size="2" face="Symbol" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&#183;</font><font size="1" style="font-size:3.0pt;">&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160; </font><font size="2" style="font-size:10.0pt;">dose responsive pharmacokinetics in humans;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt .75in;text-indent:-.25in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt .75in;text-indent:-.25in;"><font size="2" face="Symbol" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&#183;</font><font size="1" style="font-size:3.0pt;">&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160; </font><font size="2" style="font-size:10.0pt;">demonstrated reduction of PARP activity in human subjects;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt .75in;text-indent:-.25in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt .75in;text-indent:-.25in;"><font size="2" face="Symbol" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&#183;</font><font size="1" style="font-size:3.0pt;">&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160; </font><font size="2" style="font-size:10.0pt;">amenable dosage formulation for further clinical and commercial development;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt .75in;text-indent:-.25in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt .75in;text-indent:-.25in;"><font size="2" face="Symbol" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&#183;</font><font size="1" style="font-size:3.0pt;">&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160; </font><font size="2" style="font-size:10.0pt;">clinical activity with once daily oral administration as a monotherapy, including a disease control rate of 75% in a selected patient population that enrolled patients with advanced cancers using the dose that has been selected for Phase 3 studies; and</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt .75in;text-indent:-.25in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt .75in;text-indent:-.25in;"><font size="2" face="Symbol" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&#183;</font><font size="1" style="font-size:3.0pt;">&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160; </font><font size="2" style="font-size:10.0pt;">tolerability in a Phase&nbsp;1 combination trial with full doses of another chemotherapy agent, temozolomide, and a biologically active dose of niraparib.</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt .75in;text-indent:-.25in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">Based upon these key characteristics, as well as the data discussed below, we believe that niraparib has the potential to be effective in patients with solid tumors, including ovarian, breast, gastric, lung, Ewing&#146;s sarcoma and prostate cancers.</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><i><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;font-style:italic;">Niraparib Preclinical Development</font></i></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><i><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;font-style:italic;">In vitro</font></i><font size="2" style="font-size:10.0pt;">, niraparib increased the radiosensitivity of NSCLC cell lines.&#160; Furthermore, niraparib was shown to dramatically reduce PARP activity in these same cancer cell lines within two hours of treatment.&#160; In testing conducted in mice, treatment of tumor cells with niraparib resulted in the prolonged inhibition of PARP.&#160; Niraparib treatment sensitized tumor cells to subsequent radiotherapy and chemotherapy in xenograft models.&#160; As a monotherapy, niraparib inhibited the growth of tumors bearing a BRCA1 mutation.</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><i><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;font-style:italic;">Niraparib Clinical Development</font></i></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">In&nbsp;2011, Merck reported preliminary results from a two-part Phase&nbsp;1 clinical study of niraparib to determine its toxicity and tolerability, pharmacokinetic and pharmacodynamic profiles, and preliminary anti-tumor activity.&#160; Oral treatment with niraparib at doses ranging from&nbsp;30mg to&nbsp;400mg given once daily was evaluated in 60&nbsp;patients with advanced solid tumors.&#160; The first part of the clinical study was a dose escalation to establish a maximum tolerated dose.&#160; The second part of the clinical study was a dose expansion study that included patients with platinum resistant ovarian cancer and prostate cancer.&#160; The maximum tolerated dose of oral niraparib was established as 300mg daily on a continuous schedule.&#160; A&nbsp;mean plasma half-life of 40&nbsp;hours (range 37 to 42&nbsp;hours) and dose-proportional pharmacokinetics were observed.&#160; PARP inhibition of 50% or more was observed following administration of niraparib doses equal to or greater than 80mg when measured at times when plasma contained the lowest levels of the drug.&#160; Evidence of anti-tumor activity was observed in patients with&nbsp;BRCA1 and&nbsp;BRCA2 mutations and in patients with sporadic cancers.&#160; In total, there were 12&nbsp;patients with responses</font><font size="2" style="font-size:10.0pt;"> under the Response Evaluation Criteria in Solid Tumors, or RECIST, published rules</font><font size="2" style="font-size:10.0pt;">.&#160; Ten of the 12&nbsp;patients had ovarian cancer (seven BRCA-mutation carriers, three sporadic), and two of the&nbsp;12 were patients with breast cancer.&#160; In addition, stable disease, or SD, occurred in eight other patients.&#160; Four of those eight patients had ovarian cancer (two BRCA-mutation carriers), and two of the eight patients had NSCLC.&#160; Overall, 46% of ovarian cancer patients (n=47) had a clinical benefit, defined as SD for greater than 12&nbsp;weeks (21%) or partial response (26%), or&nbsp;PR.&#160; Sixty-three percent of ovarian cancer patients with a BRCA mutation (n=21) had clinical benefit, defined as SD for greater than 12&nbsp;weeks (21%) or PR (37%).&#160; In</font></p>
<p align="center" style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-align:center;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p align="center" style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-align:center;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">17<a name="PB_17_103352_7748"></a></font></p>
<div style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><hr size="3" width="100%" noshade color="#010101" align="left"></div>
</div>

<div style="font-family:Times New Roman;">
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;"><a href="#TableOfContents" title="Click to go to Table of Contents">Table of Contents</a></font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">June&nbsp;2013, we presented updated Phase 1 data at ASCO which demonstrated RECIST response rates as presented in the table below.</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<table border="0" cellspacing="0" cellpadding="0" width="80%" style="border-collapse:collapse;margin-left:.5in;width:80.0%;">

<td width="78%" valign="top" style="padding:0in 0in 0in 0in;width:78.12%;">
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt 20.0pt;text-indent:-10.0pt;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">Across   all doses</font></p>    </td>
<td width="3%" valign="bottom" style="padding:0in 0in 0in 0in;width:3.12%;">
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:1.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>    </td>
<td width="17%" valign="bottom" style="padding:0in 0in 0in 0in;width:17.5%;">
<p align="right" style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-align:right;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">50</font></p>    </td>
<td width="1%" valign="bottom" style="padding:0in 0in 0in 0in;width:1.26%;">
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">%</font></p>    </td>   </tr>  </table>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">Dose-limiting toxicities included grade&nbsp;3 fatigue in one patient with clinical progression, and grade&nbsp;3 pneumonitis and grade 4 thrombocytopenia, all of which resolved.&#160; Grade 1-2 toxicities included fatigue, anorexia, nausea and myelosuppression.&#160; Overall conclusions from this trial were that niraparib dosing was well tolerated, demonstrated linear pharmacokinetics, and provided evidence of target modulation and promising anti-tumor activity in patients with either BRCA mutated or non-BRCA mutated cancers.</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">In 2013, we met with the FDA and European Medicines Agency, or EMA, regarding our clinical development plans for niraparib in ovarian cancer patients.&#160; Based on the results of these meetings, we initiated a Phase 3 registration study for niraparib in patients with high grade serous, platinum sensitive, relapsed ovarian cancer.&#160; The Phase 3 ovarian cancer trial, which we refer to as NOVA, is an international, randomized, double-blind, multi-center trial that assesses the effectiveness of niraparib compared with placebo to delay progression following a platinum containing chemotherapy regimen.&#160; Patients will enroll into one of two independent cohorts based on germline BRCA mutation status.&#160; Within each cohort, patients will be randomized 2:1 to receive niraparib or placebo, and will be continuously treated with placebo or 300mg of niraparib until progression.&#160; The primary endpoint of this study is progression free survival.&#160; Secondary endpoints include patient reported outcomes, chemotherapy free interval length, and overall survival.&#160; The basis for this pivotal clinical study includes results from Phase 1 and Phase 2 studies with PARP inhibitor compounds, including a Phase 1 study of niraparib, that show a substantial RECIST response rate (up to 75%) in patients with ovarian cancer, together with the results of a clinical study in ovarian cancer in which another investigational PARP inhibitor, olaparib, demonstrated a progression free survival benefit when compared to placebo in the maintenance setting.&#160; We began enrolling patients in this global trial in July&nbsp;2013.</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">In 2013, we met with FDA and EMA regarding our clinical development plans for niraparib in breast cancer patients.&#160; Based on the results of these meetings, we initiated a Phase&nbsp;3 clinical trial evaluating niraparib in breast cancer patients with germline BRCA mutations in the fourth quarter of 2013, and we expect to dose the first patient in the first quarter of 2014.&#160; This Phase 3 trial, which we refer to as BRAVO, is an international, randomized, multi-center trial that assesses the effectiveness of niraparib compared with physician&#146;s choice of either eribulin, capecitabine, vinorelbine or gemcitabine to delay progression in metastatic breast cancer patients who have germline BRCA mutations.&#160; The key secondary endpoint of this trial is overall survival.&#160; The Phase 3 breast cancer trial is based upon the clinical activity of niraparib observed in a Phase 1 study of niraparib and the results of clinical studies of other investigational PARP inhibitors including olaparib and BMN-673.</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><b><i><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;font-style:italic;font-weight:bold;">TSR-011&#151;Anaplastic Lymphoma Kinase (ALK) and </font></i></b><b><i><font size="2" style="font-size:10.0pt;font-style:italic;font-weight:bold;">Tropomyosin-Related Kinase</font></i></b><b><i><font size="2" style="font-size:10.0pt;font-style:italic;font-weight:bold;"> (TRK) Inhibitor</font></i></b></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><i><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;font-style:italic;">Overview</font></i></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">TSR-011 is an investigational, orally available, small molecule inhibitor of ALK and TRK for the treatment of NSCLC and potentially other cancer indications.&#160; The TSR-011 program represents a molecularly targeted approach to treating certain cancer sub-populations of NSCLC that express ALK gene fusions or mutations that result in pathological constitutive activation of ALK, thereby enabling tumor cells to grow.&#160; Abnormal ALK and TRK proteins, or ALK and TRK expression, are also associated with sub-populations of other cancers including lymphoma, neuroblastoma, colorectal and thyroid cancer.&#160; TSR-011 was specifically designed to be selective for, bind tightly to, and inhibit the activity of the ALK protein to lead to or cause the death of cancer cells and the shrinking of tumors.&#160; In August&nbsp;2011, the FDA approved the first ALK inhibitor, developed by Pfizer&nbsp;Inc., or Pfizer, Xalkori (crizotinib), for the treatment of patients with locally advanced or metastatic NSCLC who are ALK+.&#160; To date, no TRK inhibitor has been approved for commercial use in any indication.</font></p>
<p align="center" style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-align:center;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p align="center" style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-align:center;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">18<a name="PB_18_103352_141"></a></font></p>
<div style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><hr size="3" width="100%" noshade color="#010101" align="left"></div>
</div>
<!-- SEQ.=1,FOLIO='18',FILE='C:\JMS\108199\13-25458-1\task6623666\25458-1-bk.htm',USER='108199',CD='Mar 14 04:20 2014' -->

<br clear="all" style="page-break-before:always;">
<div style="font-family:Times New Roman;">
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;"><a href="#TableOfContents" title="Click to go to Table of Contents">Table of Contents</a></font></p>
<p align="center" style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-align:center;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">Although the ALK gene is not widely expressed in adults, ALK is known to be involved in certain types of cancers, including subsets of NSCLC, neuroblastoma and lymphoma.&#160; For patients in these subsets, the ALK gene is fused to an activating partner or contains point mutations, which results in constitutive activation of ALK and the growth of cancer cells and tumor development.&#160; Inhibition of ALK in these cancer cells results in cell death and tumor growth inhibition or regression.&#160; The limited tissue distribution and expression of ALK in adult subjects means that ALK may be a good molecular target for a cancer therapeutic because an ALK inhibitor would primarily affect cancer cells and tumors.&#160; </font><font size="2" style="font-size:10.0pt;">TRK receptors include a family of three receptors with associated ligands, including nerve growth factor which binds to TRK-A.&#160; TRK gene rearrangements have been found in several tumor types, including NSCLC, papillary thyroid, colorectal and salivary cancer.&#160; </font><font size="2" style="font-size:10.0pt;">In patients with these tumors, the TRK gene is fused to an activating partner or contains point mutations, which results in constitutive activation of TRK, the growth of cancer cells and tumor development.&#160; Inhibition of TRK in these cancer cells results in cell death and tumor growth inhibition.&#160; </font><font size="2" style="font-size:10.0pt;">TRK expression in tumors is involved with tumor aggressiveness, metastatic potential, perineural invasion and pain.</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">We believe that existing commercially available diagnostic tests for the identification of ALK gene fusions will facilitate rapid and efficient development of our lead ALK/TRK inhibitor product candidate, TSR-011, for ALK-associated indications.&#160; We are working with a diagnostic company to support the development of a TRK diagnostic.&#160; In September&nbsp;2012, we filed an IND for TSR-011 with the FDA that became effective in October&nbsp;2012, and in November&nbsp;2012, we announced that we had dosed the first patient in a Phase&nbsp;1/2a dose escalation clinical trial of TSR-011 in cancer patients.&#160; Data from this trial will be used to select a dose and schedule of TSR-011.&#160; In the second half of 2013, we identified 60mg as a dose to move forward in clinical development.&#160; We also began a Phase 1 expansion study to explore fractionated doses of the 60mg daily dose of TSR-011 in patients with ALK or TRK expression, including those with ALK+ and TRK+ </font><font size="2" style="font-size:10.0pt;">NSCLC who have not been previously treated with ALK inhibitors, those with ALK+ NSCLC who have progressed during treatment with other ALK inhibitors, and those patients with other tumor types driven by ALK or TRK</font><font size="2" style="font-size:10.0pt;">.&#160; The expansion stage of the Phase&nbsp;1 clinical trial will evaluate the activity of TSR-011 in cancer patients with ALK mutations or gene fusions.&#160; Data from this study will inform us of the activity of TSR-011 in a relevant patient population, including ALK inhibitor na&#239;ve and resistant ALK+ NSCLC patients and TRK+ patients, and will be used to design clinical trials that will be used to support future regulatory submissions. </font><font size="2" style="font-size:10.0pt;">We also plan to evaluate TSR-011 in combination preclinical pharmacology studies with our immuno-oncology anti-tumor agents.</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><i><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;font-style:italic;">Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer (NSCLC)</font></i></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">According to the American Cancer Society, over 1.6&nbsp;million new lung cancer cases are identified worldwide annually, including 200,000 new lung cancer cases in the United States.&#160; Lung cancer is the leading cause of cancer death in men and the second leading cause of cancer death in women.&#160; Lung cancer is typically divided into two groups based upon the histologic appearance of the tumor cells&#151;small cell and non-small cell lung cancer, each of which is treated with distinct chemotherapeutic approaches.&#160; According to the American Cancer Society, NSCLC accounts for approximately 85% of lung cancer cases, with approximately 75% of these patients being diagnosed with metastatic or advanced disease.&#160; Despite the introduction of new therapies, such as Avastin (bevacizumab) and Alimta (pemetrexed), patients with locally advanced or metastatic NSCLC have five-year survival rates of just 24% and 4%, respectively, according to the Surveillance Epidemiology and End Results program of the National Cancer Institute.&#160; ALK is believed to be a key driver of tumor development in approximately 5% of all NSCLC patients.&#160; TRK may also drive tumor growth in patients with TRK+ NSCLC.</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><i><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;font-style:italic;">Background on ALK and TRK Inhibitors</font></i></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">There is currently one ALK inhibitor that is marketed.&#160; In 2011, Pfizer launched Xalkori (crizotinib), a dual MET/ALK inhibitor that acts as an inhibitor for mesenchymal epithelial transition tyrosine kinase, or MET, a driver of certain types of cancers, and ALK.&#160; Clinical studies demonstrated efficacy in a NSCLC sub-population expressing an ALK fusion protein.&#160; Treatment with crizotinib results in rapid tumor shrinkage in the majority of ALK patients.&#160; However, resistance mechanisms to crizotinib treatment occur within a median time frame of 10&nbsp;months.&#160; In addition, the FDA issued a &#147;Dear Doctor&#148; letter in December&nbsp;2011 related to drug induced hepatotoxicity, which now requires monitoring of elevated liver enzymes.&#160; Currently, crizotinib is dosed near its maximum tolerated dose, and side effects include severe or fatal pneumonitis, QTc prolongation and visual effects.</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">We believe there is a well-defined development and regulatory approval path for an ALK inhibitor, and that TSR-011 can be developed in a rapid and efficient manner, because appropriate patients for study can be identified with commercially available and other diagnostic tests, and testing for ALK at diagnosis is becoming more common.&#160; Based upon</font></p>
<p align="center" style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-align:center;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p align="center" style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-align:center;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">19<a name="PB_19_103352_7608"></a></font></p>
<div style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><hr size="3" width="100%" noshade color="#010101" align="left"></div>
</div>
<!-- SEQ.=1,FOLIO='19',FILE='C:\JMS\108199\13-25458-1\task6623666\25458-1-bk.htm',USER='108199',CD='Mar 14 04:20 2014' -->

<br clear="all" style="page-break-before:always;">
<div style="font-family:Times New Roman;">
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;"><a href="#TableOfContents" title="Click to go to Table of Contents">Table of Contents</a></font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">the data set forth below, we believe TSR-011 has the potential to be effective in patients progressing on crizotinib because it has activity against ALK mutations that arise in patients following treatment with crizotinib.&#160; To date, no TRK inhibitor has been approved for commercial use in any indication.</font></p>
<p align="center" style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-align:center;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><i><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;font-style:italic;">TSR-011 Preclinical Development</font></i></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">TSR-011 has demonstrated promising results in preclinical studies, and was found to be more active against the ALK protein than what is reported in other studies for crizotinib.&#160; Also in these studies, it was observed that the IC50 of TSR-011 for recombinant ALK L1196M was 0.1nM, which is 200 times less than the IC50 of crizotinib for this ALK mutant protein.&#160; IC50 is the concentration of inhibitor at which 50% of the target protein activity is inhibited.&#160; The ALK L1196M mutation has been detected in patients whose tumors progress while they are being treated with crizotinib, and is currently the most commonly identified ALK mutation observed in patients treated with crizotinib.</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">The <i>in vivo</i> activity of our ALK inhibitors has been examined in several ALK models, including an anaplastic large cell lymphoma xenograft model.&#160; Daily oral dosing resulted in statistically significant tumor growth inhibition (p &lt; 0.0001) without weight loss.&#160; The activity of ALK was evaluated in these tumors post dosing and complete inhibition of phosphorylated ALK, a marker of ALK activation, was observed.</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><i><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;font-style:italic;">TSR-011 Clinical Development</font></i></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">We plan to develop TSR-011 for oncology indications, including the treatment of patients with NSCLC whose tumors have altered ALK or TRK proteins and expression patterns.&#160; In September&nbsp;2012, we filed an IND for TSR-011 with the FDA that became effective in October&nbsp;2012, and in November&nbsp;2012, we announced that we had dosed the first patient in a Phase&nbsp;1/2a dose escalation clinical trial of TSR-011 in cancer patients.&#160; One goal of the Phase&nbsp;1 clinical trial is to determine the maximum tolerated dose of TSR-011 and to define an optimal dosing schedule.&#160; During the dose escalation phase of this trial we identified a 60mg dose for further clinical study.&#160; This total daily dose is being studied in a 60mg expansion cohort as a fractionated dose, administered as 30mg every 12 hours and 20mg every 8 hours in patients with ALK+ and TRK+ cancers.&#160; </font><font size="2" style="font-size:10.0pt;">In September&nbsp;2013, we reported that we had observed preliminary clinical activity in this study in one papillary thyroid carcinoma patient and one pancreatic cancer patient without ALK expression, and in three patients with ALK+ NCSLC who progressed following prior treatment with crizotinib.&#160; Of the three ALK+ NSCLC patients who progressed on prior crizotinib treatment, one achieved a RECIST partial response after four weeks of treatment with TSR-011; one, with disease not evaluable by RECIST criteria, achieved an investigator-assessed partial response; and one has stable disease.&#160; In addition, one patient with papillary thyroid carcinoma and one patient with pancreatic cancer each had long-term stable disease following several cycles of TSR-011 treatment.&#160; Preliminary results after eight weeks of treatment with TSR-011 demonstrated disease control (partial responses plus stable disease) in 11 of 17 (65%) evaluable patients treated with TSR-011.</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">We plan to evaluate TSR-011 in three parallel cohorts of patients: those with ALK+ NSCLC who have not been previously treated with ALK inhibitors; those with NSCLC who have progressed during treatment with other ALK inhibitors; and those with other tumor types expressing ALK or TRK.&#160; We have expanded the Phase 1/2a clinical trial of TSR-011 to multiple clinical trial sites in the United States and Europe and plan to expand further into Asia.&#160; During cohort expansion and prior to initiating a registration study, we intend to replace the Phase 1 formulation with an extended release formulation that is intended to provide convenience for patients.&#160; Information from this study may also be used to optimize future clinical trial designs that will be used to support future regulatory submissions.</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><b><i><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;font-style:italic;font-weight:bold;">Immuno-Oncology Platform</font></i></b></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><i><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;font-style:italic;">Background on Immuno-Oncology Platform</font></i></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">Antibodies to immune checkpoint receptors have recently demonstrated promise in the treatment of certain solid tumors, including metastatic melanoma, renal cell carcinoma and NSCLC.&#160; Although the normal function of immune checkpoint receptors is to maintain immune homeostasis, they are co-opted by certain tumors to evade immune surveillance.&#160; PD-1, TIM-3 and LAG-3 are each checkpoint regulators that modulate the function of the immune system via different mechanisms and, when activated and interacting with their respective ligands, may limit the ability of the immune system to respond effectively to tumors.&#160; By blocking the interaction of PD-1, TIM-3 and LAG-3 with their respective ligands, antibodies targeting these checkpoint regulators aim to restore immune anti-cancer function in patients across a variety of tumor types.&#160; We believe that therapeutic antibodies selected from the programs within our collaboration with AnaptysBio will form the basis of a strategic platform that will</font></p>
<p align="center" style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-align:center;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p align="center" style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-align:center;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">20<a name="PB_20_103352_8146"></a></font></p>
<div style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><hr size="3" width="100%" noshade color="#010101" align="left"></div>
</div>
<!-- SEQ.=1,FOLIO='20',FILE='C:\JMS\108199\13-25458-1\task6623666\25458-1-bk.htm',USER='108199',CD='Mar 14 04:20 2014' -->

<br clear="all" style="page-break-before:always;">
<div>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;"><a href="#TableOfContents" title="Click to go to Table of Contents">Table of Contents</a></font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">potentially enable us to develop novel monotherapy and combination-based approaches with immuno-oncology and other anti-cancer agents in a variety of new tumor indications, not addressed with our current product candidates, and to study combination approaches in the clinic, both with our existing product candidates and potentially with new candidates we either in-license or access through collaborative transactions with others.&#160; As further discussed below, antibody candidates from these programs are expected to enter clinical trials over the next 18 to 24 months, for example.</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><i><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;font-style:italic;">Anti-PD-1Antibodies</font></i></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">Programmed cell death protein 1 (PD-1, CD279) is a well-validated target for tumor immunotherapy.&#160; PD-1 operates as a negative regulator of T-cell function and interacts with two ligands, PD-L1 and PD-L2.&#160; Many tumor types up-regulate PD-L1 on the cell surface as a means of modulating the host immune system and avoiding anti-tumor responses.&#160; Antibodies to PD-1 have now been studied in a number of clinical trials in several tumor types including melanoma, NSCLC, prostate, renal and colorectal carcinoma.&#160; Anti-tumor responses of long duration have been noted, which may be further promoted through combination therapy with additional immuno-regulatory therapeutics.</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">AnaptysBio has generated a number of potent antibodies to PD-1 with demonstrated functionality in in vitro assays.&#160; We have selected a lead monospecific antibody for the PD-1 program, TSR-042, to which we have exclusive rights.&#160; We expect to begin clinical trials using TSR-042 in mid-2015.&#160; In addition, we plan to evaluate our immuno-oncology anti-tumor agents, such as TSR-042, in combination preclinical pharmacology studies with TSR-011, niraparib and potentially other anti-tumor agents.</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><i><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;font-style:italic;">Anti-TIM-3 and Dual-Reactive Anti-PD-1/TIM-3 Antibodies</font></i></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">T-cell immunoglobulin domain and mucin domain-3 (TIM-3), initially identified on activated Th1 cells, has been shown to be a negative regulator of the immune response.&#160; Blockade of TIM-3 promotes T-cell mediated anti-tumor immunity and has anti-tumor activity in a range of mouse tumor models.&#160; Combinations of TIM-3 blockade with other immunotherapeutic agents such as TSR-042 (our lead anti-PD-1 antibody), anti-CD137 antibodies and others, can be additive or synergistic in increasing anti-tumor effects.&#160; TIM-3 expression has been associated with a number of different tumor types including melanoma, NSCLC and renal cancer, and additionally, expression of intratumoral TIM-3 has been shown to correlate with poor prognosis across a range of tumor types including NSCLC, cervical, and gastric cancers.&#160; Blockade of TIM-3 is also of interest in promoting increased immunity to a number of chronic viral diseases.&#160; TIM-3 has also been shown to interact with a number of ligands including galectin-9, phosphatidylserine and HMGB1, although which of these, if any, are relevant in regulation of anti-tumor responses is not clear at present.</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">The aim of the TIM-3 program is to generate human or humanized antibodies to TIM-3 which have functional antagonist activity.&#160; Such activity would be expected to block the negative signaling of TIM-3, enhance T-cell responses, and promote anti-tumor immune responses.&#160; A number of fully-human anti-TIM-3 antibodies have been generated by AnaptysBio with demonstrated functional activity and to which we have exclusive rights.&#160; In addition, several dual-reactive antibodies to PD-1 and TIM-3 have been isolated and are in the process of being advanced into functional activity testing.&#160; Affinity maturation of the monospecific anti-TIM-3 antibodies is currently in progress to identify lead candidate antibodies meeting certain design goals, and, in collaboration with AnaptysBio, we expect to select a lead anti-TIM-3 candidate for clinical development during the second quarter of 2014.</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><i><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;font-style:italic;">Anti-LAG-3 and Dual-Reactive Anti-PD-1/LAG-3 Antibodies</font></i></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">Lymphocyte-activation gene-3 (LAG-3) is a CD4 related transmembrane protein expressed on activated T-cells and regulatory T-cells.&#160; Following T-cell activation and up-regulation of LAG-3, LAG-3 binds MHC Class&nbsp;II and results in down-regulation of the immune response.&#160; Affinity of LAG-3 for MHC class II is higher than that of CD4, allowing for potent dampening of T-cell activation via direct blocking of the interaction as well as direct signaling.&#160; Blockade of LAG-3 promotes T-cell mediated anti-tumor immunity and has anti-tumor activity in a range of mouse tumor models.&#160; Simultaneous blockade of LAG-3 with PD-1 appears to be synergistic with enhanced anti-tumor effects.</font></p>
<p align="center" style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-align:center;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p align="center" style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-align:center;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">21<a name="PB_21_103352_7091"></a></font></p>
<div style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><hr size="3" width="100%" noshade color="#010101" align="left"></div>
</div>
<!-- SEQ.=1,FOLIO='21',FILE='C:\JMS\108199\13-25458-1\task6623666\25458-1-bk.htm',USER='108199',CD='Mar 14 04:20 2014' -->

<br clear="all" style="page-break-before:always;">
<div style="font-family:Times New Roman;">
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;"><a href="#TableOfContents" title="Click to go to Table of Contents">Table of Contents</a></font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">The aim of the LAG-3 program is to generate human or humanized antibodies to LAG-3 that have functional antagonist activity.&#160; Such activity would be expected to block the negative signaling of LAG-3 and promote anti-tumor immune responses.&#160; Monospecific anti-LAG-3 antibodies have been generated by AnaptysBio from both mouse immunization techniques, as well as from the proprietary libraries, and those antibodies are currently being characterized and affinity matured to reach certain design goals.&#160; In addition, AnaptysBio is progressing toward screening and isolation of dual-reactive antibodies to PD-1 and LAG-3.&#160; In collaboration with AnaptysBio, we expect to select a lead anti-LAG-3 candidate for clinical development in the third quarter of 2014.</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><b><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;font-weight:bold;">Licensing Agreements</font></b></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><b><i><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;font-style:italic;font-weight:bold;">License for Rolapitant</font></i></b></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">In December&nbsp;2010, we entered into a license agreement with OPKO to obtain an exclusive, royalty bearing, sublicensable worldwide license, to research, develop, manufacture, market and sell rolapitant.&#160; The license agreement also extends to an additional, backup compound, SCH900978, to which we have the same rights and obligations as rolapitant, but which we are not currently advancing.&#160; Under the OPKO license we are obligated to use commercially reasonable efforts to conduct all preclinical, clinical, regulatory and other activities necessary to develop and commercialize rolapitant.</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">Under the terms of the OPKO license, we paid OPKO $6.0&nbsp;million upon signing the agreement and issued 1,500,000 shares of our Series&nbsp;O convertible preferred stock.&#160; We are also required to make development milestone payments to OPKO</font><font size="2" style="font-size:10.0pt;">  </font><font size="2" style="font-size:10.0pt;">of up to an aggregate of $30.0&nbsp;million if specified regulatory and initial commercial sales milestones are achieved.&#160; In addition, we are required to make additional milestone payments to OPKO of up to an aggregate of $85.0&nbsp;million if specified levels of annual net sales of rolapitant are achieved.&#160; If commercial sales of rolapitant commence, we are required to pay OPKO tiered royalties on the amount of annual net sales achieved in the United States and Europe at percentage rates that range from the low teens to the low twenties, which we expect will result in an effective royalty rate in the low teens.&#160; The royalty rate on annual net sales outside of the United States and Europe is slightly above the single digits.&#160; We will pay royalties on rolapitant until the later of the date that all of the patent rights licensed from OPKO and covering rolapitant expire, are invalidated or are not enforceable and twelve years from the first commercial sale of the product, in each case, on a country-by-country and product-by-product basis.&#160; If we elect to develop and commercialize rolapitant in Japan through a third-party licensee, we will share equally with OPKO all amounts received by us in connection with such activities under our agreement with such third party, subject to certain exceptions and deductions.&#160; OPKO also retains an option to become the exclusive distributor of such products in Latin America, provided that OPKO exercises that option within a defined period following specified regulatory approvals in the United States.</font></p>
<p align="center" style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-align:center;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">The license with OPKO will remain in force until the expiration of the royalty term in each country, unless OPKO has cause to terminate the license earlier for our material breach of the license or bankruptcy.&#160; We have a right to terminate the license at any time during the term for any reason on three months&#146; written notice to OPKO.</font></p>
<p align="center" style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-align:center;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p align="center" style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-align:center;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">22<a name="PB_22_103352_455"></a></font></p>
<div style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><hr size="3" width="100%" noshade color="#010101" align="left"></div>

<!-- SEQ.=1,FOLIO='22',FILE='C:\JMS\108199\13-25458-1\task6623666\25458-1-bk.htm',USER='108199',CD='Mar 14 04:20 2014' -->

<br clear="all" style="page-break-before:always;">
<div>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;"><a href="#TableOfContents" title="Click to go to Table of Contents">Table of Contents</a></font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><b><i><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;font-style:italic;font-weight:bold;">License for Niraparib</font></i></b></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">In May&nbsp;2012, we entered into a license agreement with Merck Sharp&nbsp;&amp; Dohme Corp., a subsidiary of Merck, under which we obtained exclusive, worldwide rights to certain patents and non-exclusive rights to certain Merck know-how, to research, develop, manufacture, market and sell niraparib and a backup compound, MK-2512, for all therapeutic and prophylactic uses in humans.&#160; We are not currently advancing MK-2512.&#160; Under the Merck license, we are obligated to use diligent efforts to develop and commercialize a licensed product.</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">Under the terms of the license agreement, we made an up-front payment to Merck of $7.0&nbsp;million in June&nbsp;2012, and upon dosing of the first patient in our Phase 3 ovarian cancer clinical trial in July&nbsp;2013, we made a milestone payment of $1.9 million.&#160; We are required to make total milestone payments to Merck of up to $57.0&nbsp;million in development and regulatory milestones for the first indication, up to $29.5&nbsp;million in development and regulatory milestones for each successive indication, and up to $87.5&nbsp;million in one-time sales milestones based on the achievement of annual sales objectives.&#160; If commercial sales of niraparib commence, we will pay Merck tiered royalties at percentage rates in the low teens based on worldwide annual net sales, until the later of the expiration of the last patent licensed from Merck covering or claiming niraparib, or the tenth anniversary of the first commercial sale of niraparib, in either case, on a country-by-country basis.</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">The license with Merck will remain in effect until the expiration of the royalty term in such country, unless terminated earlier by the mutual agreement of the parties or due to the material breach or bankruptcy of a party.&#160; In addition, beginning upon completion of the first Phase&nbsp;2 clinical trial of a licensed product candidate, we may terminate the license without cause by giving 180&nbsp;days written notice.</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><b><i><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;font-style:italic;font-weight:bold;">License for TSR-011</font></i></b></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">In March&nbsp;2011, we entered into a license agreement with Amgen, under which we received an exclusive, royalty bearing, sublicensable worldwide license under certain of Amgen&#146;s patent rights to research, develop, manufacture, market and sell licensed ALK inhibitor compounds, including TSR-011.&#160; We are also responsible for using commercially reasonable efforts to conduct all preclinical, clinical, regulatory and other activities necessary to develop and commercialize an ALK product.&#160; In the event that we wish to sublicense any of the development and commercialization rights to any third party, we are required to grant Amgen a right of first negotiation with respect to the rights we propose to sublicense.</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">Under the terms of the license agreement, we made an up-front payment to Amgen of $0.5 million, and upon dosing of the first patient in our Phase 1/2a clinical trial in October&nbsp;2012, we made a milestone payment of $1.0 million.&#160; We are required to make total milestone payments to Amgen of up to an aggregate of $138.0 million if specified clinical development, regulatory, initial commercialization and annual net product sales milestones are achieved.&#160; If commercial sales of a product commence, we will pay Amgen royalties at percentage rates ranging from the mid-single digits to slightly above the single digits based on cumulative worldwide net sales until the later of the last patent licensed from Amgen covering the product, the loss of regulatory exclusivity for the product, or the tenth anniversary of the first commercial sale of the product, in all cases, on a country-by-country and product-by-product basis.</font></p>
<p align="center" style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-align:center;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">The license with Amgen will remain in force until the expiration of the royalty term in each country, unless Amgen has cause to terminate the license earlier for our material breach of the license or bankruptcy, or in the event that we or any sublicensee bring a challenge against Amgen in relation to the licensed patents.&#160; We have the right to terminate the license with Amgen on Amgen&#146;s bankruptcy, or at any time during the term on ninety days&#146; written notice if our board of directors concludes that due to scientific, technical, regulatory or commercial reasons, the further commercialization of licensed products is no longer feasible.</font></p>
<p align="center" style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-align:center;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p align="center" style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-align:center;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">23<a name="PB_23_103352_4141"></a></font></p>

<br clear="all" style="page-break-before:always;">
<div style="font-family:Times New Roman;">
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;"><a href="#TableOfContents" title="Click to go to Table of Contents">Table of Contents</a></font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><b><i><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;font-style:italic;font-weight:bold;">License for Immuno-Oncology Platform</font></i></b></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">In March&nbsp;2014, we entered into a c</font><font size="2" style="font-size:10.0pt;">ollaboration and exclusive license agreement with AnaptysBio, a privately-held therapeutic antibody company.&#160; Under the terms of this agreement, we obtained an exclusive, royalty-bearing, sublicenseable worldwide license to research, develop, manufacture, market and sell products based on AnaptysBio&#146;s </font><font size="2" style="font-size:10.0pt;">proprietary technology for the discovery, generation and optimization of certain specified immunotherapy antibodies.&#160; Specifically, we received exclusive rights to monospecific antibody product candidates targeting TIM-3, LAG-3 and PD-1 (TSR-042) and dual-reactive antibody product candidates targeting PD-1/TIM-3 and PD-1/LAG-3.&#160; Under the agreement, AnaptysBio is responsible for performing initial discovery and development of therapeutic antibodies with the goal of generating immunotherapy antibodies for use in the treatment of cancer.&#160; We are responsible for all subsequent preclinical, clinical, regulatory, manufacturing and other activities necessary to develop and commercialize antibodies selected under each of three development programs, and we are obligated to use commercially reasonable efforts to research, develop or commercialize at least one product under each development program.</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">Under the terms of this agreement, we are obligated to make an up-front, non-creditable and non-refundable cash payment of $17.0 million to AnaptysBio.&#160; We are also required to reimburse AnaptysBio on a quarterly basis for specified costs incurred by AnaptysBio in its initial discovery and development activities covered by the agreement.&#160; For each of the three development programs, we will also be required to make milestone payments to AnaptysBio of up to $18.0 million if certain research and development milestone events are achieved, and up to an additional $90.0 million of milestone payments if certain U.S. and non-U.S. regulatory submissions and approvals occur in initial and subsequent indications.&#160; We will also be required to pay AnaptysBio tiered single-digit royalties, on a product-by-product basis, on the amount of worldwide annual net sales achieved, and additional commercial milestone payments if specified levels of annual net sales of a product are attained.</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">This agreement expires on the earliest date after which no further payments are due to AnaptysBio, unless earlier terminated.&#160; Either party may terminate the agreement in the event of an uncured material breach by the other party.&#160; We may terminate the agreement at any time upon 90 days prior written notice to AnaptysBio.</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><b><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;font-weight:bold;">Competition</font></b></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">Our industry is highly competitive and subject to rapid and significant technological change.&#160; While we believe that our development experience and scientific knowledge provide us with competitive advantages, we may face competition from large pharmaceutical and biotechnology companies, smaller pharmaceutical and biotechnology companies, including specialty pharmaceutical companies and generic drug companies, academic institutions, government agencies and research institutions, and others.</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">The acquisition or licensing of pharmaceutical products is also very competitive, and a number of more established companies, which have acknowledged strategies to in-license or acquire products, may have competitive advantages as may other emerging companies taking similar or different approaches to product acquisitions.&#160; The more established companies may have a competitive advantage over us due to their size, cash flows and institutional experience.</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">Compared to TESARO, many of our competitors may have significantly greater financial, technical and human resource capabilities.&#160; Mergers and acquisitions in the pharmaceutical and biotechnology industries may result in even more resources being concentrated among a smaller number of our competitors.&#160; Our commercial opportunity could be reduced or eliminated if our competitors develop or market products or other novel technologies that are more effective, safer or less costly than any that will be commercialized by us, or obtain regulatory approval for their products more rapidly than we may obtain approval for ours.&#160; Our success will be based in part on our ability to identify, develop, and manage a portfolio of drugs that are safer and more effective in the treatment and support of cancer patients.</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><b><i><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;font-style:italic;font-weight:bold;">Rolapitant Competition</font></i></b></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">Oral aprepitant and its IV pro-drug fosaprepitant, which are both known by the brand name EMEND and marketed by Merck, are currently the only commercially available NK-1 receptor antagonists.&#160; Helsinn Healthcare has completed Phase 3 trials of, and had an NDA accepted by the FDA for filing for, an oral combination NK-1 receptor antagonist and 5-HT3 receptor antagonist product (netupitant plus Aloxi (palonosetron HCl), known as NEPA) that would be marketed by Helsinn Healthcare and Eisai,&nbsp;Inc. for the prevention of CINV following HEC and MEC.&#160; EMA has also accepted a regulatory submission for NEPA in these same indications.</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><b><i><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;font-style:italic;font-weight:bold;">Niraparib Competition</font></i></b></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">We believe the products in development targeting the PARP pathway consist of AstraZeneca&nbsp;Plc&#146;s AZD-2281 (olaparib), BioMarin Pharmaceutical&nbsp;Inc.&#146;s BMN-673 and AbbVie&#146;s ABT-888 (veliparib), each currently in Phase 3 clinical trials, and Clovis Oncology,&nbsp;Inc.&#146;s CO-338 (rucaparib) and Eisai,&nbsp;Inc.&#146;s E-7016, each currently in Phase 2 clinical trials, and Teva Pharmaceutical Industries,&nbsp;Ltd.&#146;s CEP-9722, currently in a Phase&nbsp;1 clinical trial.&#160; AstraZeneca has announced that it has had a marketing authorization application accepted by the EMA, and expects to file a U.S. regulatory submission in 2014, for olaparib for the treatment of ovarian cancer.</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-indent:.5in;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><b><i><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;font-style:italic;font-weight:bold;">TSR-011 Competition</font></i></b></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><font size="1" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:3.0pt;">&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160;&#160; </font><font size="2" style="font-size:10.0pt;">There is currently one ALK inhibitor that is marketed, Xalkori (crizotinib), a dual MET/ALK inhibitor marketed by Pfizer.&#160; In addition, we are aware of several oral ALK inhibitors in clinical development.&#160; These products include Novartis AG&#146;s LDK378 (ceritinib), which is currently in Phase&nbsp;3 clinical trials.&#160; Novartis has submitted a regulatory application in the U.S. for LDK378, which received &#147;breakthrough therapy&#148; designation from the FDA in 2013.&#160; Other oral ALK inhibitors in clinical development include Chugai Pharmaceutical&nbsp;Co.,&nbsp;Ltd.&#146;s CH5424802 (alectinib), currently recruiting for a Phase 3</font></p>
<p align="center" style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-align:center;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">&nbsp;</font></p>
<p align="center" style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;text-align:center;"><font size="2" face="Times New Roman" style="font-size:10.0pt;">24<a name="PB_24_103646_7906"></a></font></p>
<div style="margin:0in 0in .0001pt;"><hr size="3" width="100%" noshade color="#010101" align="left"></div>
